cover.lede: Czterdzieści dwie strony o nauce przechowywania, porządku w spiżarni, dwunastu sosach, na które zasługuje każda półka — i karta przepisu na jednogarnkową fasolę, którą jadlibyśmy przez miesiąc.
cover.coverLine2: Dwanaście sosów, na które zasługuje każda półka
cover.coverLine3: Dlaczego oliwa się starzeje — i co z tym zrobić

toc.heading: Spis treści
toc.subheading: Czterdzieści dwie strony, sześć działów, jedna okładka i dwanaście przepisów wprost z dobrze prowadzonej spiżarni.

departments.weekendHeading: Pięć rzeczy do ugotowania w weekend
departments.weekendDek: Krótka lista. Bez teatru. Pięć przepisów, które dobra spiżarnia i tak chce zrobić.
departments.pantryHeading: Co w tym miesiącu trzymamy w spiżarni
departments.pantryDek: Pięć słoików, butelek i woreczków, które zasługują na półkę — z liczbami w tle.

editorsNote.label: Od redakcji
editorsNote.body: Larderlab powstał, bo kulinarny internet miał dwa rejestry — przepis dnia i hałas dietetycznego marketingu — i prawie nic między nimi. Zrobiliśmy to „między". Pismo dla osoby, która już prowadzi własną kuchnię. Witamy w numerze czwartym.

feature.leadCaption: Talerz wolno duszonej fasoli, danie z okładki — sfotografowane w naszej redakcyjnej kuchni testowej, późnym popołudniem.
feature.translationBanner: Tekst dostępny na razie po angielsku. Polskie tłumaczenie jest w opracowaniu. Oryginał poniżej.

recipeCard.title: Fasola z jednego garnka
recipeCard.ingredient1: 1 szklanka suchej białej fasoli, namoczonej przez noc
recipeCard.method1: Odsącz fasolę i przełóż do ciężkiego, żaroodpornego garnka. Zalej zimną wodą tak, by przykryła fasolę o 2 cm.
recipeCard.method2: Wciśnij połówki cebuli przekrojem do dołu, ułóż wokół czosnek i listek laurowy, na wierzch połóż skórkę cytryny.
recipeCard.method3: Wstaw do piekarnika nagrzanego do 150 °C, pod przykryciem, aż fasola będzie kremowa w środku. Sprawdź po 60 minutach, potem co 15.
recipeCard.method4: Sól dodaj dopiero pod koniec — wcześniej skórka twardnieje.
recipeCard.method5: Wyjmij cebulę i skórkę. Skrop oliwą, posyp ziołami, podawaj z grubym tostem.
recipeCard.notes: Najlepsza będzie tu szybko gotująca się fasola biała — cannellini, alubia blanca, jaś. Wywar to skarb, nie wylewaj go.
recipeCard.storage: Schłodzona, pod przykryciem, do czterech dni w lodówce. Dobrze zamraża się we własnym wywarze, do dwóch miesięcy.

pullQuote.body: Dobra spiżarnia to nie zapas. To system, który po cichu gotuje kolację, zanim zdecydujesz, czym jest kolacja.

newsletter.heading: Jeden list. Jedna karta przepisu. Niedziela.
newsletter.body: Krótki list redakcyjny, sprawdzona karta przepisu z dobrze prowadzonej spiżarni i nasze typy z półki, z weryfikacją cen.
newsletter.success: Dziękujemy. Najbliższy list jest w drodze.
newsletter.consentAfter: . Jeden list w niedzielę. Wypisz się, kiedy chcesz.

footer.leadParagraph: Larderlab to mała redakcja pisząca o architekturze spiżarni, składnikach i powolnym rzemiośle dobrze prowadzonej kuchni. Niezależna. Cytowana. Bez pieniędzy od producentów.
footer.disclaimer: Larderlab to pismo redakcyjne. Nic, co tu publikujemy, nie jest poradą medyczną ani dietetyczną. Jeśli sposób żywienia ma wpływ na twoje zdrowie, porozmawiaj z lekarzem lub dietetykiem.

about.metaDescription: Who we are, what we publish, and why we built a magazine for cooks who already run the kitchen.
body: Larderlab is a small, independent food magazine. We write about pantry architecture, ingredients, technique, and the slow craft of stocking a kitchen well.

We started Larderlab because the cooking internet had two registers — chase-the-trend recipe blogs and shouty diet talk — and almost nothing in between. So we built the in-between. A magazine for the cook who already runs the kitchen.
## What we publish

We publish features, departments, recipe cards, pantry essays, and ingredient deep-dives. Every issue closes with one cover story, several departments, and a sealed-shut envelope of recipes you can cook from a stocked pantry.
## What we don't do
We don't sell diet plans. We don't publish weight-loss numbers. We don't accept payment for editorial coverage. Our affiliate links never affect rankings.
## How to reach us
Email hello@larderlab.com. Letters to the editor are read every week.

editorialStandards.metaDescription: How Larderlab reports, sources, and edits.
body: Larderlab is independent food writing. The reader trust we want to earn is built on the same boring habits the best magazines have always practised.
## Sourcing

Every claim with a number behind it is cited. We prefer peer-reviewed papers and government food-safety guidance for shelf-life and storage; for taste, technique, and pantry strategy we rely on our own test-kitchen work, named in the byline.
## Pricing
Every product recommendation shows the price we observed, the retailer, and the date. Prices drift. We re-check on each issue.
## No payment for placement
We do not accept money from manufacturers, distributors, or PR agencies in exchange for editorial coverage.
## Affiliate links
Some product links are affiliate links. They never affect our rankings, and we disclose on every page where they appear.
## Corrections
When we are wrong, we correct in the open and timestamp the change.

methodology.metaDescription: How Larderlab develops a recipe, tests a pantry strategy, and rates an ingredient.
body: This is the editorial standards page for Larderlab — what we test, how we cite, and what we'll never do under the byline.
## Recipes

Every recipe in Larderlab is cooked at least three times in our test kitchen, by at least two different cooks, on a domestic range — not a restaurant burner. We publish active time, total time, yield, and difficulty. We publish notes when something fails.
## Pantry strategy
Pantry essays are built from food-science literature (storage temperatures, water activity, oxidation kinetics for fats) and from our own running record of what actually keeps in a real kitchen.
## Ingredient deep-dives

When we write about an ingredient — olive oil, dried beans, garam masala, a particular variety of rice — we follow a four-part frame: what is it, what's the literature on it, what's the real-world exposure or use case, and where do we land.
## Photography
Every plate photo on Larderlab is shot in our own test kitchen unless otherwise captioned. We don't dress food. We don't hide what didn't work.
## What we will not do
Sell diet plans. Publish weight-loss numbers. Accept payment for editorial placement. Use 'superfood', 'detox', 'clean', or 'miracle' under our masthead.

methodologyV12.metaDescription: The long-form spec for Larderlab's editorial method, versioned and dated.
methodologyV12.intro: Five short standards a feature has to clear before it goes into the issue. We version them like software. The page below is the long-form one.
methodologyV12.step1Body: Every recipe in Larderlab is cooked at least three times by two cooks. The recipe card on the page is the version that survived round three.
methodologyV12.step2Body: Storage temperatures, oxidation rates, salt percentages — anything with a number behind it is sourced. Taste calls are signed by the cook who made them.
methodologyV12.step3Body: We cook on a domestic range. Pots and pans you can buy. We test recipes against the pantry we describe — if the cover story names twelve sauces, the recipes lean on those twelve.
methodologyV12.step4Body: Every draft passes a voice review. No hectoring, no diet talk, no surprise upsells. The reader is a cook, not a target.
methodologyV12.step5Body: When we're wrong, the page carries a dated note at the bottom and we link to the new version.
methodologyV12.changeLog12: v1.2 · April 2026 · Recipe-card structure standardised. Plate-photo provenance now visible in caption. Added storage block to every recipe.
methodologyV12.changeLog11: v1.1 · February 2026 · Test-kitchen sign-off line introduced. Pantry strategy frame documented.
methodologyV12.changeLog10: v1.0 · January 2026 · Initial public spec — three-cook rule, sourcing requirements, the four-part ingredient frame.

pipeline.metaDescription: An open log of what we're testing, drafting, and shooting for the next issue.
pipeline.intro: An open log of every feature, recipe card, and ingredient deep-dive we're testing for the next issue. Updated each week. We share this because secrecy belongs to ad agencies.

items.title: The twelve sauces every pantry earns
Olive oil ageing, in plain English
A field guide to fermentation rules
Why your dried beans get tougher with salt

privacy.metaDescription: What we collect, what we don't, and how we handle reader data.
body: This page describes how Larderlab collects, uses, and shares information when you visit larderlab.com.
## What we collect
Standard server analytics — pages visited, referring URL, approximate location, device, browser. If you subscribe to our Sunday letter, your email address.
## What we don't collect
Your name. Your precise location. Anything that identifies you personally beyond what you give us.
## Cookies
A small number — session continuity, analytics, and a consent record. No third-party advertising cookies.
## Your rights
Access, correction, deletion, objection, portability. Email privacy@larderlab.com.
## Contact
privacy@larderlab.com.

terms.metaDescription: The terms that apply when you use larderlab.com.
body: By using larderlab.com you agree to these terms.
## Editorial, not advice

Larderlab publishes editorial food writing. Nothing on this site is medical or nutrition advice for a specific condition. If a meal plan is going to interact with your health, talk to a doctor or registered dietitian.
## Accuracy
We work hard to be accurate. We cite. We update. But we are not infallible. Content is provided as-is.
## Affiliate links
Some links earn us a commission. They never affect our editorial rankings.
## Intellectual property
All editorial content is owned by Larderlab or licensed to us. You may share excerpts with attribution.
## Contact
hello@larderlab.com.

affiliateDisclosure.metaDescription: How affiliate links work on Larderlab and what they do — and don't — affect.
body: Larderlab participates in a small number of affiliate programs. Some product links earn us a commission when you buy through them, at no additional cost to you.
## How we use them

We link to products we recommend on their merits, not their commission rate. We disclose on every page that includes affiliate links. We test top picks against alternatives we don't earn commission from.
## Programs
Amazon Associates, ShareASale, Awin, and direct programs with selected suppliers.
## Why we're OK with it
Affiliate links align our interests with yours: we only earn when we recommend something you actually buy and find useful.
If you have concerns, email hello@larderlab.com.

contact.metaDescription: How to reach us — corrections, story tips, recipe feedback.
body: ## General
hello@larderlab.com — feedback, story tips, pantry questions, everything else.
## Corrections
corrections@larderlab.com — spotted something wrong? We want to know. We respond within five working days and publish corrections in the open.
## Recipe feedback
recipes@larderlab.com — cooked one of ours? Tell us how it landed in your kitchen. The next issue's notes block is built on letters from this address.
## Privacy
privacy@larderlab.com — anything GDPR or CCPA-related.
## Response times
We are a small team. We read everything. We answer most letters within five working days.

newsletter.metaDescription: One short editorial letter, one tested recipe card, every Sunday.
body: One short editorial letter, one tested recipe card you can cook from a stocked pantry, and the picks we'd put on the shelf this week. No fire-hose, no hype, no upsells. Sundays only.

errorPage.body: Strona zwróciła błąd. Mamy go w logach. Spróbuj ponownie albo wróć na okładkę.

notFound.body: Tej strony nie znajdujemy. Może zgubiła się między numerami. Wróć na okładkę albo wybierz dział poniżej.

peptide.heroH1: Przemyślana spiżarnia dla osób stosujących peptydy.
peptide.heroDek: Stosy suplementów, architektura spiżarni i realna matematyka kosztów — dla osób na terapii peptydami. Cytowane badania. Bez sponsoringu.
peptide.dietitianBadge: Zweryfikowane przez dr Mayę Rao, RDN — żywienie w terapii peptydami
peptide.educationalBanner: Treści Larderlab mają charakter edukacyjny. Wskazówki dotyczące spiżarni, makro i suplementów nie zastępują porady lekarskiej. Skonsultuj się z lekarzem przed rozpoczęciem, przerwaniem lub łączeniem suplementu z terapią peptydami.
